content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission SUPERSEDED
by: 92-094

February 3, 1992


Lyle L. Lough, Chairman
Lassen County Board of Supervisors
Administration Building
707 Nevada Street
Susanville, CA  96130

                                    Re:  Your Request for Advice
                                         Our File No. A-92-75

Dear Mr. Lough:

     This is in response to your request for advice concerning
your duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

     You inquire whether you have a conflict of interest under the
Act if, in your capacity as a member of the Lassen County Board of
Supervisors, you vote on a proposed state prison expansion which
affects the California Correctional Center (the "Center") in
Susanville. You indicate that your business has supplied services
in the form of water and soil testing in the approximate value of
$5,000 to that state facility as late as June 1991.

     You indicated to me by telephone on January 24, 1992, that in
all of your previous contracts with the Center, you have been an
independent contractor and not a salaried employee of the Center.
Furthermore, you stated that the proposed vote on prison expansion
by the Lassen County Board of Supervisors will directly affect
whether expansion of the Center will occur.  Finally, you
indicated that if the expansion occurs it is likely that the
Center will need more soil and water testing and that you will bid
for those contracts.


_________________________
     ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-92-75
Page 2


     Generally, public officials are prohibited from making,
participating in making or using their official position to
influence government decisions which will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on them, their family
members, or on their financial interests. (See Sections 87100 and
87103.)

     Based upon the facts you have provided, you would be
prohibited from participating in the decision by the Lassen County
Board of Supervisors on whether to expand the facilities of the
California Correctional Center in Susanville.

     We reach this conclusion because you indicate that the vote
on prison expansion by the Lassen County Board of Supervisors will
directly affect whether the Center will expand; if the expansion
occurs the Center likely will need more soil and water testing;
and you will bid on any soil or water testing contract offered by
the Center as a result of the expansion.² As such, we believe
that it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on you as set forth in Sections 87100
and 87103.

     Attached for your reference is a general summary of the Act's
conflict-of-interest requirements and the Commission's opinion in
In re Thorner (1 FPPC Ops. 198, Opinion No. 75-089), which we
believe is relevant to the issue of whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that you will receive a financial benefit from the
decision regarding prison expansion.

     Should you have any questions, please contact me at (916)
322-5901.


                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

SH:aa

Attachment




_________________________
     ² Based upon your previous contracts, it appears that the
potential financial benefits of a new contract to you would exceed
$250. If it would be less than $250, then you would not be
prohibited from participating in the prison expansion decision.
(See Regulation 18702.1(a)(4).)
